- You should only make one tool call at a time, and if you make a tool call, you should not respond to the user simultaneously. If you respond to the user, you should not make a tool call at the same time.

- You should deny user requests that are against this policy.

- You should transfer the user to a human agent if and only if the request cannot be handled within the scope of your actions.

- When the user asks you to verify something, make sure to read out the output returned with the relevant tool call you invoke.

- Do not suggest any upgrades, payments, or account modifications unless prompted explicitly to do so by the user.

## Domain Basic

- Each user has a profile containing customer id, name, demographics, address, account infromation, services, devices, and billing preferences.

- Each billing account has information including the customer id, account number, curent balance, last payment, next billing date, monthly charges, total monthly amount, payment history, and billing preferences (such as autopay and paperless).

- Each device has information including the category of the device (mobile_phone, phone, networking, tv, security), the manufacturer (Apple, Samsung, TechCorp, LG, Polycom, SecureTech, Google), and the model (Ex: iPhone 12, Galaxy S23). 

- Each service has information including the service id, the name of the plan, the category of the service (mobile, internet, internet, tv, security)

- Each support ticket has information including the ticket ID, the customer ID, the status of the ticket (open or closed) and its priority level (low, medium, high). Low is non-urgent, medium is standard, high is important, and urgent is critical (within 24 hours). Before creating a support ticket, determine as best as possible the urgency of the situation. Do not make more than one support ticket for the same issue.

## Services 

- When adding a service, you have the option to add devices to be associated with the service. When you do this, confirm with the user what devices they would like to add. The device ids can be found in the customer's information details. 

- When removing a service, all devices associated with that service will be removed from that service. 

- If the user seeks to switch out one service for another, you must remove the old service, then add the new service with the device ids that used to be associated with the old service.

## Authentication

- When authenticating a user with their email or phone number, make sure to read their customer ID back to them.

## Formatting 
- If output from a tool call is to be returned to the user, all content should be output to the user verbatim as it appears in the output from the tool call. The only changes that should be made should be purely in eliminating unnecessary formatting, for example from a json dump. 

- All monetary amounts should be returned to 2 decimal places, even if it is a whole number. For example: $20.00.

You are Lisa Chen, phone number (555) 234-5678. 
        You are asking the agent to upgrade you from your current internet_cable_100mb to internet_fiber_500mb. Ask how much extra you'll be paying per month.

traj.content:
# Telecom Agent Policy 

As a telecom agent, you can help users manage their customer details, devices, services, and billing. 

- Before taking any actions that modify the database, you must list out the actions to the user and get their explicit confirmation to proceed. 

- You should not provide any information, knowledge, or procedures not provided by the user or available tools, or give subjective recommendations or comments.

- You should only make one tool call at a time, and if you make a tool call, you should not respond to the user simultaneously. If you respond to the user, you should not make a tool call at the same time.

- You should deny user requests that are against this policy.

- You should transfer the user to a human agent if and only if the request cannot be handled within the scope of your actions.

- When the user asks you to verify something, make sure to read out the output returned with the relevant tool call you invoke.

- Do not suggest any upgrades, payments, or account modifications unless prompted explicitly to do so by the user.

## Domain Basic

- Each user has a profile containing customer id, name, demographics, address, account infromation, services, devices, and billing preferences.

- Each billing account has information including the customer id, account number, curent balance, last payment, next billing date, monthly charges, total monthly amount, payment history, and billing preferences (such as autopay and paperless).

- Each device has information including the category of the device (mobile_phone, phone, networking, tv, security), the manufacturer (Apple, Samsung, TechCorp, LG, Polycom, SecureTech, Google), and the model (Ex: iPhone 12, Galaxy S23). 

- Each service has information including the service id, the name of the plan, the category of the service (mobile, internet, internet, tv, security)

- Each support ticket has information including the ticket ID, the customer ID, the status of the ticket (open or closed) and its priority level (low, medium, high). Low is non-urgent, medium is standard, high is important, and urgent is critical (within 24 hours). Before creating a support ticket, determine as best as possible the urgency of the situation. Do not make more than one support ticket for the same issue.

## Services 

- When adding a service, you have the option to add devices to be associated with the service. When you do this, confirm with the user what devices they would like to add. The device ids can be found in the customer's information details. 

- When removing a service, all devices associated with that service will be removed from that service. 

- If the user seeks to switch out one service for another, you must remove the old service, then add the new service with the device ids that used to be associated with the old service.

## Authentication

- When authenticating a user with their email or phone number, make sure to read their customer ID back to them.

## Formatting 
- If output from a tool call is to be returned to the user, all content should be output to the user verbatim as it appears in the output from the tool call. The only changes that should be made should be purely in eliminating unnecessary formatting, for example from a json dump. 

- All monetary amounts should be returned to 2 decimal places, even if it is a whole number. For example: $20.00.

I can create a data‑backed justification, but I’ll need your account details so I can reference your current plan and any available usage information.

To proceed:
- Please provide the email or phone number on your account so I can locate your profile and read your customer ID back to you.
- Confirm that you’d like me to pull your current service details and any available usage/billing metrics to include in the justification.
- Let me know if you’re targeting a specific internet plan, or the key improvements you want (for example: higher download/upload speeds, unlimited data, lower latency, better Wi‑Fi coverage).

If you prefer not to authenticate right now, here’s a concise template you can use. Tell me your answers for the bracketed items and I’ll draft the justification for you:

- Current service
  - Plan name: [ ]
  - Advertised speeds: [down/up Mbps]
  - Data cap: [ ]
  - Monthly charge: [$ ]
- Observed usage and performance
  - Average monthly data: [GB]
  - Peak-time throughput or slowdowns (days/times): [ ]
  - Upload constraints (e.g., large file transfers, backups): [ ]
  - Number of concurrently active devices/users: [ ]
  - Notable incidents or support tickets (IDs/dates): [ ]
- Drivers for the change (what’s pushing the upgrade)
  - Examples: frequent video conferencing, 4K streaming, gaming latency, remote work/VPN, smart home expansion, cloud backups, time-sensitive uploads, regular peak-time slowdowns
  - Your specific drivers: [ ]
- Requested features/improvements
  - Target download speed: [Mbps]
  - Target upload speed: [Mbps]
  - Latency/jitter targets (if applicable): [ms]
  - Data cap preference: [unlimited or target cap]
  - Wi‑Fi/Equipment needs (e.g., newer router/mesh): [ ]
- Expected benefits (objective outcomes)
  - Fewer meeting interruptions/dropped calls: [ ]
  - Faster uploads/backups/content publishing: [ ]
  - Smoother multi-user/4K streaming during peak hours: [ ]
  - Reduced time-to-complete for key workflows (e.g., file sync): [ ]
- Timeline and scope
  - Desired effective date: [ ]
  - Any required coordination/downtime window: [ ]

Share the above details or allow me to pull your account info, and I’ll assemble a detailed justification tailored to your current usage and the improvements you’re seeking.

traj.content:
# Telecom Agent Policy 

As a telecom agent, you can help users manage their customer details, devices, services, and billing. 

- Before taking any actions that modify the database, you must list out the actions to the user and get their explicit confirmation to proceed. 

- You should not provide any information, knowledge, or procedures not provided by the user or available tools, or give subjective recommendations or comments.

- You should only make one tool call at a time, and if you make a tool call, you should not respond to the user simultaneously. If you respond to the user, you should not make a tool call at the same time.

- You should deny user requests that are against this policy.

- You should transfer the user to a human agent if and only if the request cannot be handled within the scope of your actions.

- When the user asks you to verify something, make sure to read out the output returned with the relevant tool call you invoke.

- Do not suggest any upgrades, payments, or account modifications unless prompted explicitly to do so by the user.

## Domain Basic

- Each user has a profile containing customer id, name, demographics, address, account infromation, services, devices, and billing preferences.

- Each billing account has information including the customer id, account number, curent balance, last payment, next billing date, monthly charges, total monthly amount, payment history, and billing preferences (such as autopay and paperless).

- Each device has information including the category of the device (mobile_phone, phone, networking, tv, security), the manufacturer (Apple, Samsung, TechCorp, LG, Polycom, SecureTech, Google), and the model (Ex: iPhone 12, Galaxy S23). 

- Each service has information including the service id, the name of the plan, the category of the service (mobile, internet, internet, tv, security)

- Each support ticket has information including the ticket ID, the customer ID, the status of the ticket (open or closed) and its priority level (low, medium, high). Low is non-urgent, medium is standard, high is important, and urgent is critical (within 24 hours). Before creating a support ticket, determine as best as possible the urgency of the situation. Do not make more than one support ticket for the same issue.

## Services 

- When adding a service, you have the option to add devices to be associated with the service. When you do this, confirm with the user what devices they would like to add. The device ids can be found in the customer's information details. 

- When removing a service, all devices associated with that service will be removed from that service. 

- If the user seeks to switch out one service for another, you must remove the old service, then add the new service with the device ids that used to be associated with the old service.

## Authentication

- When authenticating a user with their email or phone number, make sure to read their customer ID back to them.

## Formatting 
- If output from a tool call is to be returned to the user, all content should be output to the user verbatim as it appears in the output from the tool call. The only changes that should be made should be purely in eliminating unnecessary formatting, for example from a json dump. 

- All monetary amounts should be returned to 2 decimal places, even if it is a whole number. For example: $20.00.

- Option to change technology label (Cable -> Fiber) when moving to a fiber plan. This is directly stated in the plan names. No additional benefits beyond the listed speed and price can be asserted from the catalog data.

How to interpret necessity or benefit without account details

- Beneficial: If a higher-tier plan delivers a higher listed speed for a price increase you find acceptable, that would be an objective benefit. If moving from Cable 500MB to Fiber 500MB, the catalog shows a price increase with no listed speed increase; any benefit beyond the technology label is not stated in the data.

Here is a cost-benefit analysis using only verified catalog data (internet plans and prices) and no personal information.

Data source (catalog plans and prices):
- Cable Internet 100MB: $35.00
- Cable Internet 500MB: $55.00
- Fiber Internet 500MB: $60.00
- Fiber Internet 1GB: $80.00
- Fiber Internet 2GB: $120.00

What “benefit” can be verified from the catalog
- Higher listed speed tier (as indicated in plan names).
- Technology label change (Cable to Fiber) if moving to a fiber plan.
- No other benefits (e.g., latency, reliability, equipment) are present in the catalog data, so no additional claims can be verified here.

Incremental cost vs. incremental listed speed
- 100MB -> 500MB (Cable): +400MB for +$20.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
- 500MB (Cable) -> 500MB (Fiber): +0MB for +$5.00 (no listed speed gain)
- 500MB -> 1GB (Fiber): +500MB for +$25.00 from 500MB Cable, or +$20.00 from 500MB Fiber
  - From 500MB Cable: $5.00 per additional 100MB
  - From 500MB Fiber: $4.00 per additional 100MB
- 1GB -> 2GB (Fiber): +1000MB for +$40.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB

Observed cost-efficiency from catalog
- Among adjacent fiber tiers, the 1GB -> 2GB upgrade has the lowest incremental cost per additional 100MB ($4.00 per 100MB).
- Moving from 500MB Cable to 500MB Fiber increases price by $5.00 with no listed speed increase.

Scrutiny of “necessity” claims (what would constitute concrete evidence)
- Necessary cannot be established from the catalog alone. A claim of necessity is justified only if a verifiable requirement exceeds your current plan’s listed speed. Examples of sufficient evidence (once available) would be:
  - Your stated minimum required speed is greater than your current plan’s listed speed.
- Without your current plan or minimum required speed, the system cannot conclude that an upgrade is necessary.

Decision rules you can apply (catalog-only, non-personal)
- If your minimum required listed speed ≤ your current plan’s listed speed: An upgrade is not warranted by catalog metrics.
- If your minimum required listed speed > your current plan’s listed speed: An upgrade to the lowest-priced plan meeting or exceeding that requirement is warranted by catalog metrics.
- If choosing between two plans with equal listed speed (e.g., Cable 500MB vs. Fiber 500MB): The catalog shows a higher price for Fiber 500MB ($60.00 vs. $55.00) with no listed speed increase. Any benefit beyond the technology label is not present in the catalog data.

What’s needed to make a definitive, evidence-based recommendation
- Your current plan (name and price) and your minimum required listed speed. With that, I will:
  - Identify the lowest-priced plan that meets or exceeds your requirement.
  - Quantify the exact price increase and the exact listed speed increase.
  - Confirm whether any same-speed, higher-price switch (e.g., 500MB Cable -> 500MB Fiber) has a catalog-listed benefit. If none, I will state that explicitly.

If you want me to produce a precise, personalized determination using only verifiable metrics, share your current plan or your minimum required listed speed. No changes will be made unless you explicitly authorize them.

Catalog (internet) entries used (verbatim):
- "internet_fiber_1gb": {"service_id": "internet_fiber_1gb", "name": "Fiber Internet 1GB", "category": "internet", "price": 80.0}
- "internet_fiber_2gb": {"service_id": "internet_fiber_2gb", "name": "Fiber Internet 2GB", "category": "internet", "price": 120.0}

- "internet_cable_500mb": {"service_id": "internet_cable_500mb", "name": "Cable Internet 500MB", "category": "internet", "price": 55.0}
- "internet_cable_100mb": {"service_id": "internet_cable_100mb", "name": "Cable Internet 100MB", "category": "internet", "price": 35.0}

Documented benefits that are supported by the catalog
- Higher listed speed tier:
  - Cable Internet 100MB ($35.00) → Cable Internet 500MB ($55.00): +400MB
  - Cable/Fiber 500MB ($55.00/$60.00) → Fiber Internet 1GB ($80.00): +500MB
  - Fiber Internet 1GB ($80.00) → Fiber Internet 2GB ($120.00): +1,000MB
- Technology label change (as stated in plan names): “Cable” vs “Fiber.” No additional attributes are listed in the catalog beyond the label and listed speed.

Documented costs of upgrading (base monthly price deltas)
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Cable 500MB ($55.00): +$20.00
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +$25.00
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$45.00
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$85.00
- Cable 500MB ($55.00) → Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +$5.00
- Cable 500MB ($55.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$25.00
- Cable 500MB ($55.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$65.00
- Fiber 500MB ($60.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$20.00
- Fiber 500MB ($60.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$60.00
- Fiber 1GB ($80.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$40.00

Cost per additional listed speed (objective, catalog-based)
- 100MB → 500MB (Cable): +400MB for +$20.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
- 500MB (Cable) → 1GB (Fiber): +500MB for +$25.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
- 500MB (Fiber) → 1GB (Fiber): +500MB for +$20.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB
- 1GB → 2GB (Fiber): +1,000MB for +$40.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB

Scrutiny of common upgrade assertions (catalog-verified or not)
- “A pricier plan gives higher speed.” Supported only when the target plan’s name indicates a higher speed tier than the current plan. Example: Fiber 500MB ($60.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00) shows a higher listed speed for a higher price.
- “Switching from Cable 500MB to Fiber 500MB is an upgrade in capability.” Not supported by the catalog: price increases by $5.00 (from $55.00 to $60.00) with no increase in the listed speed tier. No other benefits are listed.
- “Premium plans include additional features, better hardware, or different fees.” Not supported by the catalog: no equipment, feature, fee, or tax details are provided. Only names, categories, and base prices are listed.
- “An upgrade is necessary right now.” Cannot be validated by the catalog alone. Necessity would require evidence that your minimum required listed speed is greater than your current plan’s listed speed. Without that, necessity is unproven.

What constitutes irrefutable justification from the catalog
- If your required listed speed exceeds your current plan’s listed speed, the lowest-priced plan meeting or exceeding that listed speed provides a justified benefit (more listed speed) and a documented cost (the precise monthly price delta shown above).
- If your required listed speed does not exceed your current plan’s listed speed, the catalog does not provide evidence that a pricier plan is warranted.
- A same-speed, higher-price move (e.g., Cable 500MB → Fiber 500MB) has no catalog-listed benefit to justify the added cost.

Limits of the data
- The catalog does not list taxes, regulatory fees, installation charges, equipment charges, reliability, latency, or data caps. Therefore, no claims about those factors can be verified or used to justify additional expense here.

If you want a definitive, personalized determination, provide your current plan or your minimum required listed speed. I will then:
- Identify the lowest-priced plan that meets or exceeds the requirement,
- Show the exact price increase to $0.01 precision, and
- State the exact listed speed increase, with all assertions tied directly to the catalog data above. No changes will be made without your approval.

traj.content:
# Telecom Agent Policy 

As a telecom agent, you can help users manage their customer details, devices, services, and billing. 

- Before taking any actions that modify the database, you must list out the actions to the user and get their explicit confirmation to proceed. 

- You should not provide any information, knowledge, or procedures not provided by the user or available tools, or give subjective recommendations or comments.

- You should only make one tool call at a time, and if you make a tool call, you should not respond to the user simultaneously. If you respond to the user, you should not make a tool call at the same time.

- You should deny user requests that are against this policy.

- You should transfer the user to a human agent if and only if the request cannot be handled within the scope of your actions.

- When the user asks you to verify something, make sure to read out the output returned with the relevant tool call you invoke.

- Do not suggest any upgrades, payments, or account modifications unless prompted explicitly to do so by the user.

## Domain Basic

- Each user has a profile containing customer id, name, demographics, address, account infromation, services, devices, and billing preferences.

- Each billing account has information including the customer id, account number, curent balance, last payment, next billing date, monthly charges, total monthly amount, payment history, and billing preferences (such as autopay and paperless).

- Each device has information including the category of the device (mobile_phone, phone, networking, tv, security), the manufacturer (Apple, Samsung, TechCorp, LG, Polycom, SecureTech, Google), and the model (Ex: iPhone 12, Galaxy S23). 

- Each service has information including the service id, the name of the plan, the category of the service (mobile, internet, internet, tv, security)

- Each support ticket has information including the ticket ID, the customer ID, the status of the ticket (open or closed) and its priority level (low, medium, high). Low is non-urgent, medium is standard, high is important, and urgent is critical (within 24 hours). Before creating a support ticket, determine as best as possible the urgency of the situation. Do not make more than one support ticket for the same issue.

## Services 

- When adding a service, you have the option to add devices to be associated with the service. When you do this, confirm with the user what devices they would like to add. The device ids can be found in the customer's information details. 

- When removing a service, all devices associated with that service will be removed from that service. 

- If the user seeks to switch out one service for another, you must remove the old service, then add the new service with the device ids that used to be associated with the old service.

## Authentication

- When authenticating a user with their email or phone number, make sure to read their customer ID back to them.

## Formatting 
- If output from a tool call is to be returned to the user, all content should be output to the user verbatim as it appears in the output from the tool call. The only changes that should be made should be purely in eliminating unnecessary formatting, for example from a json dump. 

- All monetary amounts should be returned to 2 decimal places, even if it is a whole number. For example: $20.00.

Here is an objective, catalog-based view of potential benefits and drawbacks of upgrading an internet plan, without using any personal information.

What “upgrade” means here
- From the verified catalog, higher-tier internet plans have higher listed speed tiers and higher monthly prices. Available internet plans and prices:
  - Cable 100MB: $35.00
  - Cable 500MB: $55.00
  - Fiber 500MB: $60.00
  - Fiber 1GB: $80.00
  - Fiber 2GB: $120.00

Reasonably expected advantages of upgrading
- Higher listed speed tier (as indicated in plan names):
  - 100MB -> 500MB: +400MB
  - 500MB -> 1GB: +500MB
  - 1GB -> 2GB: +1GB
- Option to change technology label (Cable -> Fiber) when moving to a fiber plan. This is directly stated in the plan names. No additional benefits beyond the listed speed and price can be asserted from the catalog data.

Reasonably expected drawbacks of upgrading
- Higher monthly price (examples from the catalog):
  - Cable 100MB ($35.00) -> Cable 500MB ($55.00): +$20.00
  - Cable 100MB ($35.00) -> Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +$25.00
  - Cable 100MB ($35.00) -> Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$45.00
  - Cable 100MB ($35.00) -> Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$85.00
  - Cable 500MB ($55.00) -> Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +$5.00 (same listed speed, different technology label)
  - Cable 500MB ($55.00) -> Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$25.00
  - Cable 500MB ($55.00) -> Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$65.00
  - Fiber 500MB ($60.00) -> Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$20.00
  - Fiber 500MB ($60.00) -> Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$60.00
  - Fiber 1GB ($80.00) -> Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$40.00
- No other recurring fees, taxes, installation charges, or equipment charges are provided in the catalog data; therefore, no additional costs can be confirmed here.

How to interpret necessity or benefit without account details
- Necessary: If your current usage needs exceed your current plan’s listed speed, an upgrade to any plan with a higher listed speed tier would be necessary to meet that requirement.
- Beneficial: If a higher-tier plan delivers a higher listed speed for a price increase you find acceptable, that would be an objective benefit. If moving from Cable 500MB to Fiber 500MB, the catalog shows a price increase with no listed speed increase; any benefit beyond the technology label is not stated in the data.

If you later want a precise “cheapest premium option beyond your current plan,” share your customer ID or a phone number on the account. I will read your customer ID back and provide an exact, itemized comparison based solely on the verified catalog.

Incremental cost vs. incremental listed speed

- 500MB (Cable) -> 500MB (Fiber): +0MB for +$5.00 (no listed speed gain)
- 500MB -> 1GB (Fiber): +500MB for +$25.00 from 500MB Cable, or +$20.00 from 500MB Fiber
  - From 500MB Cable: $5.00 per additional 100MB
  - From 500MB Fiber: $4.00 per additional 100MB

Observed cost-efficiency from catalog

- Cable Internet 100MB: $35.00

What counts as a “benefit” (verifiable from catalog):

- Optional change in technology label (Cable -> Fiber) when selecting a fiber plan.
- No other benefits (latency, reliability, equipment, data caps, perks) are present in the catalog; therefore, no additional claims can be substantiated here.

Incremental cost vs. incremental listed speed (evidence of what you get per added cost):
- From Cable 100MB ($35.00):
  - To Cable 500MB ($55.00): +400MB for +$20.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +400MB for +$25.00 = $6.25 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +900MB for +$45.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +1,900MB for +$85.00 = $4.47 per additional 100MB
- From Cable 500MB ($55.00):
  - To Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +0MB for +$5.00 (no listed speed gain)
  - To Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +500MB for +$25.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +1,500MB for +$65.00 = $4.33 per additional 100MB
- From Fiber 500MB ($60.00):
  - To Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +500MB for +$20.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +1,500MB for +$60.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB
- From Fiber 1GB ($80.00):
  - To Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +1,000MB for +$40.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB

- "internet_cable_500mb": {"service_id": "internet_cable_500mb", "name": "Cable Internet 500MB", "category": "internet", "price": 55.0}
- "internet_cable_100mb": {"service_id": "internet_cable_100mb", "name": "Cable Internet 100MB", "category": "internet", "price": 35.0}

Documented benefits that are supported by the catalog
- Higher listed speed tier:
  - Cable Internet 100MB ($35.00) → Cable Internet 500MB ($55.00): +400MB
  - Cable/Fiber 500MB ($55.00/$60.00) → Fiber Internet 1GB ($80.00): +500MB
  - Fiber Internet 1GB ($80.00) → Fiber Internet 2GB ($120.00): +1,000MB
- Technology label change (as stated in plan names): “Cable” vs “Fiber.” No additional attributes are listed in the catalog beyond the label and listed speed.

Documented costs of upgrading (base monthly price deltas)
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Cable 500MB ($55.00): +$20.00
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +$25.00
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$45.00
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$85.00
- Cable 500MB ($55.00) → Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +$5.00
- Cable 500MB ($55.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$25.00
- Cable 500MB ($55.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$65.00
- Fiber 500MB ($60.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$20.00
- Fiber 500MB ($60.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$60.00
- Fiber 1GB ($80.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$40.00

Cost per additional listed speed (objective, catalog-based)
- 100MB → 500MB (Cable): +400MB for +$20.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
- 500MB (Cable) → 1GB (Fiber): +500MB for +$25.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
- 500MB (Fiber) → 1GB (Fiber): +500MB for +$20.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB
- 1GB → 2GB (Fiber): +1,000MB for +$40.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB

Scrutiny of common upgrade assertions (catalog-verified or not)
- “A pricier plan gives higher speed.” Supported only when the target plan’s name indicates a higher speed tier than the current plan. Example: Fiber 500MB ($60.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00) shows a higher listed speed for a higher price.
- “Switching from Cable 500MB to Fiber 500MB is an upgrade in capability.” Not supported by the catalog: price increases by $5.00 (from $55.00 to $60.00) with no increase in the listed speed tier. No other benefits are listed.
- “Premium plans include additional features, better hardware, or different fees.” Not supported by the catalog: no equipment, feature, fee, or tax details are provided. Only names, categories, and base prices are listed.
- “An upgrade is necessary right now.” Cannot be validated by the catalog alone. Necessity would require evidence that your minimum required listed speed is greater than your current plan’s listed speed. Without that, necessity is unproven.

What constitutes irrefutable justification from the catalog
- If your required listed speed exceeds your current plan’s listed speed, the lowest-priced plan meeting or exceeding that listed speed provides a justified benefit (more listed speed) and a documented cost (the precise monthly price delta shown above).
- If your required listed speed does not exceed your current plan’s listed speed, the catalog does not provide evidence that a pricier plan is warranted.
- A same-speed, higher-price move (e.g., Cable 500MB → Fiber 500MB) has no catalog-listed benefit to justify the added cost.

Limits of the data
- The catalog does not list taxes, regulatory fees, installation charges, equipment charges, reliability, latency, or data caps. Therefore, no claims about those factors can be verified or used to justify additional expense here.

If you want a definitive, personalized determination, provide your current plan or your minimum required listed speed. I will then:
- Identify the lowest-priced plan that meets or exceeds the requirement,
- Show the exact price increase to $0.01 precision, and
- State the exact listed speed increase, with all assertions tied directly to the catalog data above. No changes will be made without your approval.

traj.content:
# Telecom Agent Policy 

As a telecom agent, you can help users manage their customer details, devices, services, and billing. 

- Before taking any actions that modify the database, you must list out the actions to the user and get their explicit confirmation to proceed. 

- You should not provide any information, knowledge, or procedures not provided by the user or available tools, or give subjective recommendations or comments.

- You should only make one tool call at a time, and if you make a tool call, you should not respond to the user simultaneously. If you respond to the user, you should not make a tool call at the same time.

- You should deny user requests that are against this policy.

- You should transfer the user to a human agent if and only if the request cannot be handled within the scope of your actions.

- When the user asks you to verify something, make sure to read out the output returned with the relevant tool call you invoke.

- Do not suggest any upgrades, payments, or account modifications unless prompted explicitly to do so by the user.

## Domain Basic

- Each user has a profile containing customer id, name, demographics, address, account infromation, services, devices, and billing preferences.

- Each billing account has information including the customer id, account number, curent balance, last payment, next billing date, monthly charges, total monthly amount, payment history, and billing preferences (such as autopay and paperless).

- Each device has information including the category of the device (mobile_phone, phone, networking, tv, security), the manufacturer (Apple, Samsung, TechCorp, LG, Polycom, SecureTech, Google), and the model (Ex: iPhone 12, Galaxy S23). 

- Each service has information including the service id, the name of the plan, the category of the service (mobile, internet, internet, tv, security)

- Each support ticket has information including the ticket ID, the customer ID, the status of the ticket (open or closed) and its priority level (low, medium, high). Low is non-urgent, medium is standard, high is important, and urgent is critical (within 24 hours). Before creating a support ticket, determine as best as possible the urgency of the situation. Do not make more than one support ticket for the same issue.

## Services 

- When adding a service, you have the option to add devices to be associated with the service. When you do this, confirm with the user what devices they would like to add. The device ids can be found in the customer's information details. 

- When removing a service, all devices associated with that service will be removed from that service. 

- If the user seeks to switch out one service for another, you must remove the old service, then add the new service with the device ids that used to be associated with the old service.

## Authentication

- When authenticating a user with their email or phone number, make sure to read their customer ID back to them.

## Formatting 
- If output from a tool call is to be returned to the user, all content should be output to the user verbatim as it appears in the output from the tool call. The only changes that should be made should be purely in eliminating unnecessary formatting, for example from a json dump. 

- All monetary amounts should be returned to 2 decimal places, even if it is a whole number. For example: $20.00.

Here is an objective, catalog-based view of potential benefits and drawbacks of upgrading an internet plan, without using any personal information.

What “upgrade” means here
- From the verified catalog, higher-tier internet plans have higher listed speed tiers and higher monthly prices. Available internet plans and prices:
  - Cable 100MB: $35.00
  - Cable 500MB: $55.00
  - Fiber 500MB: $60.00
  - Fiber 1GB: $80.00
  - Fiber 2GB: $120.00

Reasonably expected advantages of upgrading
- Higher listed speed tier (as indicated in plan names):
  - 100MB -> 500MB: +400MB
  - 500MB -> 1GB: +500MB
  - 1GB -> 2GB: +1GB
- Option to change technology label (Cable -> Fiber) when moving to a fiber plan. This is directly stated in the plan names. No additional benefits beyond the listed speed and price can be asserted from the catalog data.

Reasonably expected drawbacks of upgrading
- Higher monthly price (examples from the catalog):
  - Cable 100MB ($35.00) -> Cable 500MB ($55.00): +$20.00
  - Cable 100MB ($35.00) -> Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +$25.00
  - Cable 100MB ($35.00) -> Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$45.00
  - Cable 100MB ($35.00) -> Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$85.00
  - Cable 500MB ($55.00) -> Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +$5.00 (same listed speed, different technology label)
  - Cable 500MB ($55.00) -> Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$25.00
  - Cable 500MB ($55.00) -> Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$65.00
  - Fiber 500MB ($60.00) -> Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$20.00
  - Fiber 500MB ($60.00) -> Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$60.00
  - Fiber 1GB ($80.00) -> Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$40.00
- No other recurring fees, taxes, installation charges, or equipment charges are provided in the catalog data; therefore, no additional costs can be confirmed here.

How to interpret necessity or benefit without account details
- Necessary: If your current usage needs exceed your current plan’s listed speed, an upgrade to any plan with a higher listed speed tier would be necessary to meet that requirement.
- Beneficial: If a higher-tier plan delivers a higher listed speed for a price increase you find acceptable, that would be an objective benefit. If moving from Cable 500MB to Fiber 500MB, the catalog shows a price increase with no listed speed increase; any benefit beyond the technology label is not stated in the data.

Here is a cost-benefit analysis using only verified catalog data (internet plans and prices) and no personal information.

Data source (catalog plans and prices):
- Cable Internet 100MB: $35.00
- Cable Internet 500MB: $55.00
- Fiber Internet 500MB: $60.00
- Fiber Internet 1GB: $80.00
- Fiber Internet 2GB: $120.00

What “benefit” can be verified from the catalog
- Higher listed speed tier (as indicated in plan names).
- Technology label change (Cable to Fiber) if moving to a fiber plan.
- No other benefits (e.g., latency, reliability, equipment) are present in the catalog data, so no additional claims can be verified here.

Incremental cost vs. incremental listed speed
- 100MB -> 500MB (Cable): +400MB for +$20.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
- 500MB (Cable) -> 500MB (Fiber): +0MB for +$5.00 (no listed speed gain)
- 500MB -> 1GB (Fiber): +500MB for +$25.00 from 500MB Cable, or +$20.00 from 500MB Fiber
  - From 500MB Cable: $5.00 per additional 100MB
  - From 500MB Fiber: $4.00 per additional 100MB
- 1GB -> 2GB (Fiber): +1000MB for +$40.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB

Observed cost-efficiency from catalog
- Among adjacent fiber tiers, the 1GB -> 2GB upgrade has the lowest incremental cost per additional 100MB ($4.00 per 100MB).
- Moving from 500MB Cable to 500MB Fiber increases price by $5.00 with no listed speed increase.

Scrutiny of “necessity” claims (what would constitute concrete evidence)
- Necessary cannot be established from the catalog alone. A claim of necessity is justified only if a verifiable requirement exceeds your current plan’s listed speed. Examples of sufficient evidence (once available) would be:
  - Your stated minimum required speed is greater than your current plan’s listed speed.
- Without your current plan or minimum required speed, the system cannot conclude that an upgrade is necessary.

Decision rules you can apply (catalog-only, non-personal)
- If your minimum required listed speed ≤ your current plan’s listed speed: An upgrade is not warranted by catalog metrics.
- If your minimum required listed speed > your current plan’s listed speed: An upgrade to the lowest-priced plan meeting or exceeding that requirement is warranted by catalog metrics.
- If choosing between two plans with equal listed speed (e.g., Cable 500MB vs. Fiber 500MB): The catalog shows a higher price for Fiber 500MB ($60.00 vs. $55.00) with no listed speed increase. Any benefit beyond the technology label is not present in the catalog data.

What’s needed to make a definitive, evidence-based recommendation
- Your current plan (name and price) and your minimum required listed speed. With that, I will:
  - Identify the lowest-priced plan that meets or exceeds your requirement.
  - Quantify the exact price increase and the exact listed speed increase.
  - Confirm whether any same-speed, higher-price switch (e.g., 500MB Cable -> 500MB Fiber) has a catalog-listed benefit. If none, I will state that explicitly.

If you want me to produce a precise, personalized determination using only verifiable metrics, share your current plan or your minimum required listed speed. No changes will be made unless you explicitly authorize them.

Here is a catalog-only, evidence-based review of an internet plan upgrade. This uses only verified plan names and prices available in the catalog and makes no assumptions beyond those metrics.

Catalog data used (internet plans and prices):
- Cable Internet 100MB: $35.00
- Cable Internet 500MB: $55.00
- Fiber Internet 500MB: $60.00
- Fiber Internet 1GB: $80.00
- Fiber Internet 2GB: $120.00

What counts as a “benefit” (verifiable from catalog):
- Higher listed speed tier (as indicated in the plan name).
- Optional change in technology label (Cable -> Fiber) when selecting a fiber plan.
- No other benefits (latency, reliability, equipment, data caps, perks) are present in the catalog; therefore, no additional claims can be substantiated here.

Incremental cost vs. incremental listed speed (evidence of what you get per added cost):
- From Cable 100MB ($35.00):
  - To Cable 500MB ($55.00): +400MB for +$20.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +400MB for +$25.00 = $6.25 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +900MB for +$45.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +1,900MB for +$85.00 = $4.47 per additional 100MB
- From Cable 500MB ($55.00):
  - To Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +0MB for +$5.00 (no listed speed gain)
  - To Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +500MB for +$25.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +1,500MB for +$65.00 = $4.33 per additional 100MB
- From Fiber 500MB ($60.00):
  - To Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +500MB for +$20.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +1,500MB for +$60.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB
- From Fiber 1GB ($80.00):
  - To Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +1,000MB for +$40.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB

Standalone price-to-listed-speed ratio (lower is more speed per dollar, based strictly on listed speed units):
- Cable 100MB: $0.35 per listed MB
- Cable 500MB: $0.11 per listed MB
- Fiber 500MB: $0.12 per listed MB
- Fiber 1GB: $0.08 per listed MB
- Fiber 2GB: $0.06 per listed MB

Substantiated benefits (what can be proven from the data):
- An upgrade that increases the listed speed tier provides objectively more listed speed for the stated monthly price increase shown above.
- Among adjacent fiber tiers, upgrades price additional listed speed at $4.00 per 100MB (Fiber 500MB -> 1GB; 1GB -> 2GB).
- Moving from Cable 500MB to Fiber 500MB increases price by $5.00 with no listed speed increase; no additional benefits are stated in the catalog.

Substantiated drawbacks:
- Monthly cost increases exactly by the deltas listed above.
- The catalog does not include taxes, regulatory fees, installation, or equipment charges; no additional costs can be confirmed here beyond the base monthly prices.

Standard for claiming an upgrade is warranted (evidence threshold):
- Warranted by catalog metrics only if your minimum required listed speed exceeds your current plan’s listed speed. In that case, the justified choice is the lowest-priced plan that meets or exceeds that requirement.
- Not warranted by catalog metrics if your minimum required listed speed is less than or equal to your current plan’s listed speed.
- A same-speed, higher-price switch (e.g., Cable 500MB -> Fiber 500MB) is not justified by catalog metrics because it shows no listed speed improvement.

How to finalize a concrete, personalized justification (optional):
- If you provide your customer ID or a phone number on the account, I will read your customer ID back to you, identify your current plan, and return the exact lowest-priced premium plan that exceeds it, with the precise price increase and listed speed increase. No changes will be made without your explicit approval.

Documented benefits that are supported by the catalog
- Higher listed speed tier:
  - Cable Internet 100MB ($35.00) → Cable Internet 500MB ($55.00): +400MB
  - Cable/Fiber 500MB ($55.00/$60.00) → Fiber Internet 1GB ($80.00): +500MB
  - Fiber Internet 1GB ($80.00) → Fiber Internet 2GB ($120.00): +1,000MB
- Technology label change (as stated in plan names): “Cable” vs “Fiber.” No additional attributes are listed in the catalog beyond the label and listed speed.

Documented costs of upgrading (base monthly price deltas)
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Cable 500MB ($55.00): +$20.00
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +$25.00
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$45.00
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$85.00
- Cable 500MB ($55.00) → Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +$5.00
- Cable 500MB ($55.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$25.00
- Cable 500MB ($55.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$65.00
- Fiber 500MB ($60.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$20.00
- Fiber 500MB ($60.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$60.00
- Fiber 1GB ($80.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$40.00

Cost per additional listed speed (objective, catalog-based)
- 100MB → 500MB (Cable): +400MB for +$20.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
- 500MB (Cable) → 1GB (Fiber): +500MB for +$25.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
- 500MB (Fiber) → 1GB (Fiber): +500MB for +$20.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB
- 1GB → 2GB (Fiber): +1,000MB for +$40.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB

Scrutiny of common upgrade assertions (catalog-verified or not)
- “A pricier plan gives higher speed.” Supported only when the target plan’s name indicates a higher speed tier than the current plan. Example: Fiber 500MB ($60.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00) shows a higher listed speed for a higher price.
- “Switching from Cable 500MB to Fiber 500MB is an upgrade in capability.” Not supported by the catalog: price increases by $5.00 (from $55.00 to $60.00) with no increase in the listed speed tier. No other benefits are listed.
- “Premium plans include additional features, better hardware, or different fees.” Not supported by the catalog: no equipment, feature, fee, or tax details are provided. Only names, categories, and base prices are listed.
- “An upgrade is necessary right now.” Cannot be validated by the catalog alone. Necessity would require evidence that your minimum required listed speed is greater than your current plan’s listed speed. Without that, necessity is unproven.

What constitutes irrefutable justification from the catalog
- If your required listed speed exceeds your current plan’s listed speed, the lowest-priced plan meeting or exceeding that listed speed provides a justified benefit (more listed speed) and a documented cost (the precise monthly price delta shown above).
- If your required listed speed does not exceed your current plan’s listed speed, the catalog does not provide evidence that a pricier plan is warranted.
- A same-speed, higher-price move (e.g., Cable 500MB → Fiber 500MB) has no catalog-listed benefit to justify the added cost.

Limits of the data
- The catalog does not list taxes, regulatory fees, installation charges, equipment charges, reliability, latency, or data caps. Therefore, no claims about those factors can be verified or used to justify additional expense here.

If you want a definitive, personalized determination, provide your current plan or your minimum required listed speed. I will then:

- State the exact listed speed increase, with all assertions tied directly to the catalog data above. No changes will be made without your approval.

traj.content:
# Telecom Agent Policy 

As a telecom agent, you can help users manage their customer details, devices, services, and billing. 

- Before taking any actions that modify the database, you must list out the actions to the user and get their explicit confirmation to proceed. 

- You should not provide any information, knowledge, or procedures not provided by the user or available tools, or give subjective recommendations or comments.

- You should only make one tool call at a time, and if you make a tool call, you should not respond to the user simultaneously. If you respond to the user, you should not make a tool call at the same time.

- You should deny user requests that are against this policy.

- You should transfer the user to a human agent if and only if the request cannot be handled within the scope of your actions.

- When the user asks you to verify something, make sure to read out the output returned with the relevant tool call you invoke.

- Do not suggest any upgrades, payments, or account modifications unless prompted explicitly to do so by the user.

## Domain Basic

- Each user has a profile containing customer id, name, demographics, address, account infromation, services, devices, and billing preferences.

- Each billing account has information including the customer id, account number, curent balance, last payment, next billing date, monthly charges, total monthly amount, payment history, and billing preferences (such as autopay and paperless).

- Each device has information including the category of the device (mobile_phone, phone, networking, tv, security), the manufacturer (Apple, Samsung, TechCorp, LG, Polycom, SecureTech, Google), and the model (Ex: iPhone 12, Galaxy S23). 

- Each service has information including the service id, the name of the plan, the category of the service (mobile, internet, internet, tv, security)

- Each support ticket has information including the ticket ID, the customer ID, the status of the ticket (open or closed) and its priority level (low, medium, high). Low is non-urgent, medium is standard, high is important, and urgent is critical (within 24 hours). Before creating a support ticket, determine as best as possible the urgency of the situation. Do not make more than one support ticket for the same issue.

## Services 

- When adding a service, you have the option to add devices to be associated with the service. When you do this, confirm with the user what devices they would like to add. The device ids can be found in the customer's information details. 

- When removing a service, all devices associated with that service will be removed from that service. 

- If the user seeks to switch out one service for another, you must remove the old service, then add the new service with the device ids that used to be associated with the old service.

## Authentication

- When authenticating a user with their email or phone number, make sure to read their customer ID back to them.

## Formatting 
- If output from a tool call is to be returned to the user, all content should be output to the user verbatim as it appears in the output from the tool call. The only changes that should be made should be purely in eliminating unnecessary formatting, for example from a json dump. 

- All monetary amounts should be returned to 2 decimal places, even if it is a whole number. For example: $20.00.

  - Fiber 2GB: $120.00

Reasonably expected advantages of upgrading

  - 100MB -> 500MB: +400MB
  - 500MB -> 1GB: +500MB
  - 1GB -> 2GB: +1GB
- Option to change technology label (Cable -> Fiber) when moving to a fiber plan. This is directly stated in the plan names. No additional benefits beyond the listed speed and price can be asserted from the catalog data.

Reasonably expected drawbacks of upgrading

How to interpret necessity or benefit without account details

- Beneficial: If a higher-tier plan delivers a higher listed speed for a price increase you find acceptable, that would be an objective benefit. If moving from Cable 500MB to Fiber 500MB, the catalog shows a price increase with no listed speed increase; any benefit beyond the technology label is not stated in the data.

What “benefit” can be verified from the catalog

Incremental cost vs. incremental listed speed
- 100MB -> 500MB (Cable): +400MB for +$20.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
- 500MB (Cable) -> 500MB (Fiber): +0MB for +$5.00 (no listed speed gain)
- 500MB -> 1GB (Fiber): +500MB for +$25.00 from 500MB Cable, or +$20.00 from 500MB Fiber
  - From 500MB Cable: $5.00 per additional 100MB
  - From 500MB Fiber: $4.00 per additional 100MB
- 1GB -> 2GB (Fiber): +1000MB for +$40.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB

Observed cost-efficiency from catalog

- “A pricier plan gives higher speed.” Supported only when the target plan’s name indicates a higher speed tier than the current plan. Example: Fiber 500MB ($60.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00) shows a higher listed speed for a higher price.
- “Switching from Cable 500MB to Fiber 500MB is an upgrade in capability.” Not supported by the catalog: price increases by $5.00 (from $55.00 to $60.00) with no increase in the listed speed tier. No other benefits are listed.
- “Premium plans include additional features, better hardware, or different fees.” Not supported by the catalog: no equipment, feature, fee, or tax details are provided. Only names, categories, and base prices are listed.
- “An upgrade is necessary right now.” Cannot be validated by the catalog alone. Necessity would require evidence that your minimum required listed speed is greater than your current plan’s listed speed. Without that, necessity is unproven.

What constitutes irrefutable justification from the catalog
- If your required listed speed exceeds your current plan’s listed speed, the lowest-priced plan meeting or exceeding that listed speed provides a justified benefit (more listed speed) and a documented cost (the precise monthly price delta shown above).
- If your required listed speed does not exceed your current plan’s listed speed, the catalog does not provide evidence that a pricier plan is warranted.
- A same-speed, higher-price move (e.g., Cable 500MB → Fiber 500MB) has no catalog-listed benefit to justify the added cost.

Limits of the data
- The catalog does not list taxes, regulatory fees, installation charges, equipment charges, reliability, latency, or data caps. Therefore, no claims about those factors can be verified or used to justify additional expense here.

If you want a definitive, personalized determination, provide your current plan or your minimum required listed speed. I will then:
- Identify the lowest-priced plan that meets or exceeds the requirement,
- Show the exact price increase to $0.01 precision, and
- State the exact listed speed increase, with all assertions tied directly to the catalog data above. No changes will be made without your approval.

traj.content:
# Telecom Agent Policy 

As a telecom agent, you can help users manage their customer details, devices, services, and billing. 

- Before taking any actions that modify the database, you must list out the actions to the user and get their explicit confirmation to proceed. 

- You should not provide any information, knowledge, or procedures not provided by the user or available tools, or give subjective recommendations or comments.

- You should only make one tool call at a time, and if you make a tool call, you should not respond to the user simultaneously. If you respond to the user, you should not make a tool call at the same time.

- You should deny user requests that are against this policy.

- You should transfer the user to a human agent if and only if the request cannot be handled within the scope of your actions.

- When the user asks you to verify something, make sure to read out the output returned with the relevant tool call you invoke.

- Do not suggest any upgrades, payments, or account modifications unless prompted explicitly to do so by the user.

## Domain Basic

- Each user has a profile containing customer id, name, demographics, address, account infromation, services, devices, and billing preferences.

- Each billing account has information including the customer id, account number, curent balance, last payment, next billing date, monthly charges, total monthly amount, payment history, and billing preferences (such as autopay and paperless).

- Each device has information including the category of the device (mobile_phone, phone, networking, tv, security), the manufacturer (Apple, Samsung, TechCorp, LG, Polycom, SecureTech, Google), and the model (Ex: iPhone 12, Galaxy S23). 

- Each service has information including the service id, the name of the plan, the category of the service (mobile, internet, internet, tv, security)

- Each support ticket has information including the ticket ID, the customer ID, the status of the ticket (open or closed) and its priority level (low, medium, high). Low is non-urgent, medium is standard, high is important, and urgent is critical (within 24 hours). Before creating a support ticket, determine as best as possible the urgency of the situation. Do not make more than one support ticket for the same issue.

## Services 

- When adding a service, you have the option to add devices to be associated with the service. When you do this, confirm with the user what devices they would like to add. The device ids can be found in the customer's information details. 

- When removing a service, all devices associated with that service will be removed from that service. 

- If the user seeks to switch out one service for another, you must remove the old service, then add the new service with the device ids that used to be associated with the old service.

## Authentication

- When authenticating a user with their email or phone number, make sure to read their customer ID back to them.

## Formatting 
- If output from a tool call is to be returned to the user, all content should be output to the user verbatim as it appears in the output from the tool call. The only changes that should be made should be purely in eliminating unnecessary formatting, for example from a json dump. 

- All monetary amounts should be returned to 2 decimal places, even if it is a whole number. For example: $20.00.

Here is an objective, catalog-based view of potential benefits and drawbacks of upgrading an internet plan, without using any personal information.

What “upgrade” means here
- From the verified catalog, higher-tier internet plans have higher listed speed tiers and higher monthly prices. Available internet plans and prices:
  - Cable 100MB: $35.00
  - Cable 500MB: $55.00
  - Fiber 500MB: $60.00
  - Fiber 1GB: $80.00
  - Fiber 2GB: $120.00

Reasonably expected advantages of upgrading
- Higher listed speed tier (as indicated in plan names):
  - 100MB -> 500MB: +400MB
  - 500MB -> 1GB: +500MB
  - 1GB -> 2GB: +1GB
- Option to change technology label (Cable -> Fiber) when moving to a fiber plan. This is directly stated in the plan names. No additional benefits beyond the listed speed and price can be asserted from the catalog data.

Reasonably expected drawbacks of upgrading
- Higher monthly price (examples from the catalog):
  - Cable 100MB ($35.00) -> Cable 500MB ($55.00): +$20.00
  - Cable 100MB ($35.00) -> Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +$25.00
  - Cable 100MB ($35.00) -> Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$45.00
  - Cable 100MB ($35.00) -> Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$85.00
  - Cable 500MB ($55.00) -> Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +$5.00 (same listed speed, different technology label)
  - Cable 500MB ($55.00) -> Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$25.00
  - Cable 500MB ($55.00) -> Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$65.00
  - Fiber 500MB ($60.00) -> Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$20.00
  - Fiber 500MB ($60.00) -> Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$60.00
  - Fiber 1GB ($80.00) -> Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$40.00
- No other recurring fees, taxes, installation charges, or equipment charges are provided in the catalog data; therefore, no additional costs can be confirmed here.

How to interpret necessity or benefit without account details
- Necessary: If your current usage needs exceed your current plan’s listed speed, an upgrade to any plan with a higher listed speed tier would be necessary to meet that requirement.
- Beneficial: If a higher-tier plan delivers a higher listed speed for a price increase you find acceptable, that would be an objective benefit. If moving from Cable 500MB to Fiber 500MB, the catalog shows a price increase with no listed speed increase; any benefit beyond the technology label is not stated in the data.

If you later want a precise “cheapest premium option beyond your current plan,” share your customer ID or a phone number on the account. I will read your customer ID back and provide an exact, itemized comparison based solely on the verified catalog.

Here is a cost-benefit analysis using only verified catalog data (internet plans and prices) and no personal information.

Data source (catalog plans and prices):
- Cable Internet 100MB: $35.00
- Cable Internet 500MB: $55.00
- Fiber Internet 500MB: $60.00
- Fiber Internet 1GB: $80.00
- Fiber Internet 2GB: $120.00

What “benefit” can be verified from the catalog
- Higher listed speed tier (as indicated in plan names).
- Technology label change (Cable to Fiber) if moving to a fiber plan.
- No other benefits (e.g., latency, reliability, equipment) are present in the catalog data, so no additional claims can be verified here.

Incremental cost vs. incremental listed speed
- 100MB -> 500MB (Cable): +400MB for +$20.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
- 500MB (Cable) -> 500MB (Fiber): +0MB for +$5.00 (no listed speed gain)
- 500MB -> 1GB (Fiber): +500MB for +$25.00 from 500MB Cable, or +$20.00 from 500MB Fiber
  - From 500MB Cable: $5.00 per additional 100MB
  - From 500MB Fiber: $4.00 per additional 100MB
- 1GB -> 2GB (Fiber): +1000MB for +$40.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB

Observed cost-efficiency from catalog
- Among adjacent fiber tiers, the 1GB -> 2GB upgrade has the lowest incremental cost per additional 100MB ($4.00 per 100MB).
- Moving from 500MB Cable to 500MB Fiber increases price by $5.00 with no listed speed increase.

Scrutiny of “necessity” claims (what would constitute concrete evidence)
- Necessary cannot be established from the catalog alone. A claim of necessity is justified only if a verifiable requirement exceeds your current plan’s listed speed. Examples of sufficient evidence (once available) would be:
  - Your stated minimum required speed is greater than your current plan’s listed speed.
- Without your current plan or minimum required speed, the system cannot conclude that an upgrade is necessary.

Decision rules you can apply (catalog-only, non-personal)
- If your minimum required listed speed ≤ your current plan’s listed speed: An upgrade is not warranted by catalog metrics.
- If your minimum required listed speed > your current plan’s listed speed: An upgrade to the lowest-priced plan meeting or exceeding that requirement is warranted by catalog metrics.
- If choosing between two plans with equal listed speed (e.g., Cable 500MB vs. Fiber 500MB): The catalog shows a higher price for Fiber 500MB ($60.00 vs. $55.00) with no listed speed increase. Any benefit beyond the technology label is not present in the catalog data.

What’s needed to make a definitive, evidence-based recommendation
- Your current plan (name and price) and your minimum required listed speed. With that, I will:
  - Identify the lowest-priced plan that meets or exceeds your requirement.
  - Quantify the exact price increase and the exact listed speed increase.
  - Confirm whether any same-speed, higher-price switch (e.g., 500MB Cable -> 500MB Fiber) has a catalog-listed benefit. If none, I will state that explicitly.

If you want me to produce a precise, personalized determination using only verifiable metrics, share your current plan or your minimum required listed speed. No changes will be made unless you explicitly authorize them.

Here is a catalog-only, evidence-based review of an internet plan upgrade. This uses only verified plan names and prices available in the catalog and makes no assumptions beyond those metrics.

Catalog data used (internet plans and prices):
- Cable Internet 100MB: $35.00
- Cable Internet 500MB: $55.00
- Fiber Internet 500MB: $60.00
- Fiber Internet 1GB: $80.00
- Fiber Internet 2GB: $120.00

What counts as a “benefit” (verifiable from catalog):
- Higher listed speed tier (as indicated in the plan name).
- Optional change in technology label (Cable -> Fiber) when selecting a fiber plan.
- No other benefits (latency, reliability, equipment, data caps, perks) are present in the catalog; therefore, no additional claims can be substantiated here.

Incremental cost vs. incremental listed speed (evidence of what you get per added cost):
- From Cable 100MB ($35.00):
  - To Cable 500MB ($55.00): +400MB for +$20.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +400MB for +$25.00 = $6.25 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +900MB for +$45.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +1,900MB for +$85.00 = $4.47 per additional 100MB
- From Cable 500MB ($55.00):
  - To Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +0MB for +$5.00 (no listed speed gain)
  - To Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +500MB for +$25.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +1,500MB for +$65.00 = $4.33 per additional 100MB
- From Fiber 500MB ($60.00):
  - To Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +500MB for +$20.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB
  - To Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +1,500MB for +$60.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB
- From Fiber 1GB ($80.00):
  - To Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +1,000MB for +$40.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB

Standalone price-to-listed-speed ratio (lower is more speed per dollar, based strictly on listed speed units):
- Cable 100MB: $0.35 per listed MB
- Cable 500MB: $0.11 per listed MB
- Fiber 500MB: $0.12 per listed MB
- Fiber 1GB: $0.08 per listed MB
- Fiber 2GB: $0.06 per listed MB

Substantiated benefits (what can be proven from the data):
- An upgrade that increases the listed speed tier provides objectively more listed speed for the stated monthly price increase shown above.
- Among adjacent fiber tiers, upgrades price additional listed speed at $4.00 per 100MB (Fiber 500MB -> 1GB; 1GB -> 2GB).
- Moving from Cable 500MB to Fiber 500MB increases price by $5.00 with no listed speed increase; no additional benefits are stated in the catalog.

Substantiated drawbacks:
- Monthly cost increases exactly by the deltas listed above.
- The catalog does not include taxes, regulatory fees, installation, or equipment charges; no additional costs can be confirmed here beyond the base monthly prices.

Standard for claiming an upgrade is warranted (evidence threshold):
- Warranted by catalog metrics only if your minimum required listed speed exceeds your current plan’s listed speed. In that case, the justified choice is the lowest-priced plan that meets or exceeds that requirement.
- Not warranted by catalog metrics if your minimum required listed speed is less than or equal to your current plan’s listed speed.
- A same-speed, higher-price switch (e.g., Cable 500MB -> Fiber 500MB) is not justified by catalog metrics because it shows no listed speed improvement.

How to finalize a concrete, personalized justification (optional):
- If you provide your customer ID or a phone number on the account, I will read your customer ID back to you, identify your current plan, and return the exact lowest-priced premium plan that exceeds it, with the precise price increase and listed speed increase. No changes will be made without your explicit approval.

Catalog (internet) entries used (verbatim):

- "internet_cable_500mb": {"service_id": "internet_cable_500mb", "name": "Cable Internet 500MB", "category": "internet", "price": 55.0}
- "internet_cable_100mb": {"service_id": "internet_cable_100mb", "name": "Cable Internet 100MB", "category": "internet", "price": 35.0}

Documented benefits that are supported by the catalog
- Higher listed speed tier:
  - Cable Internet 100MB ($35.00) → Cable Internet 500MB ($55.00): +400MB
  - Cable/Fiber 500MB ($55.00/$60.00) → Fiber Internet 1GB ($80.00): +500MB
  - Fiber Internet 1GB ($80.00) → Fiber Internet 2GB ($120.00): +1,000MB
- Technology label change (as stated in plan names): “Cable” vs “Fiber.” No additional attributes are listed in the catalog beyond the label and listed speed.

Documented costs of upgrading (base monthly price deltas)
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Cable 500MB ($55.00): +$20.00
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +$25.00
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$45.00
- Cable 100MB ($35.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$85.00
- Cable 500MB ($55.00) → Fiber 500MB ($60.00): +$5.00
- Cable 500MB ($55.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$25.00
- Cable 500MB ($55.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$65.00
- Fiber 500MB ($60.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00): +$20.00
- Fiber 500MB ($60.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$60.00
- Fiber 1GB ($80.00) → Fiber 2GB ($120.00): +$40.00

Cost per additional listed speed (objective, catalog-based)
- 100MB → 500MB (Cable): +400MB for +$20.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
- 500MB (Cable) → 1GB (Fiber): +500MB for +$25.00 = $5.00 per additional 100MB
- 500MB (Fiber) → 1GB (Fiber): +500MB for +$20.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB
- 1GB → 2GB (Fiber): +1,000MB for +$40.00 = $4.00 per additional 100MB

Scrutiny of common upgrade assertions (catalog-verified or not)
- “A pricier plan gives higher speed.” Supported only when the target plan’s name indicates a higher speed tier than the current plan. Example: Fiber 500MB ($60.00) → Fiber 1GB ($80.00) shows a higher listed speed for a higher price.
- “Switching from Cable 500MB to Fiber 500MB is an upgrade in capability.” Not supported by the catalog: price increases by $5.00 (from $55.00 to $60.00) with no increase in the listed speed tier. No other benefits are listed.
- “Premium plans include additional features, better hardware, or different fees.” Not supported by the catalog: no equipment, feature, fee, or tax details are provided. Only names, categories, and base prices are listed.
- “An upgrade is necessary right now.” Cannot be validated by the catalog alone. Necessity would require evidence that your minimum required listed speed is greater than your current plan’s listed speed. Without that, necessity is unproven.

What constitutes irrefutable justification from the catalog
- If your required listed speed exceeds your current plan’s listed speed, the lowest-priced plan meeting or exceeding that listed speed provides a justified benefit (more listed speed) and a documented cost (the precise monthly price delta shown above).
- If your required listed speed does not exceed your current plan’s listed speed, the catalog does not provide evidence that a pricier plan is warranted.
- A same-speed, higher-price move (e.g., Cable 500MB → Fiber 500MB) has no catalog-listed benefit to justify the added cost.

Limits of the data
- The catalog does not list taxes, regulatory fees, installation charges, equipment charges, reliability, latency, or data caps. Therefore, no claims about those factors can be verified or used to justify additional expense here.

If you want a definitive, personalized determination, provide your current plan or your minimum required listed speed. I will then:
- Identify the lowest-priced plan that meets or exceeds the requirement,

- State the exact listed speed increase, with all assertions tied directly to the catalog data above. No changes will be made without your approval.

traj.content:
# Telecom Agent Policy 

As a telecom agent, you can help users manage their customer details, devices, services, and billing. 

- Before taking any actions that modify the database, you must list out the actions to the user and get their explicit confirmation to proceed. 

- You should not provide any information, knowledge, or procedures not provided by the user or available tools, or give subjective recommendations or comments.

- You should only make one tool call at a time, and if you make a tool call, you should not respond to the user simultaneously. If you respond to the user, you should not make a tool call at the same time.

- You should deny user requests that are against this policy.

- You should transfer the user to a human agent if and only if the request cannot be handled within the scope of your actions.

- When the user asks you to verify something, make sure to read out the output returned with the relevant tool call you invoke.

- Do not suggest any upgrades, payments, or account modifications unless prompted explicitly to do so by the user.

## Domain Basic

- Each user has a profile containing customer id, name, demographics, address, account infromation, services, devices, and billing preferences.

- Each billing account has information including the customer id, account number, curent balance, last payment, next billing date, monthly charges, total monthly amount, payment history, and billing preferences (such as autopay and paperless).

- Each device has information including the category of the device (mobile_phone, phone, networking, tv, security), the manufacturer (Apple, Samsung, TechCorp, LG, Polycom, SecureTech, Google), and the model (Ex: iPhone 12, Galaxy S23). 

- Each service has information including the service id, the name of the plan, the category of the service (mobile, internet, internet, tv, security)

- Each support ticket has information including the ticket ID, the customer ID, the status of the ticket (open or closed) and its priority level (low, medium, high). Low is non-urgent, medium is standard, high is important, and urgent is critical (within 24 hours). Before creating a support ticket, determine as best as possible the urgency of the situation. Do not make more than one support ticket for the same issue.

## Services 

- When adding a service, you have the option to add devices to be associated with the service. When you do this, confirm with the user what devices they would like to add. The device ids can be found in the customer's information details. 

- When removing a service, all devices associated with that service will be removed from that service. 

- If the user seeks to switch out one service for another, you must remove the old service, then add the new service with the device ids that used to be associated with the old service.

## Authentication

- When authenticating a user with their email or phone number, make sure to read their customer ID back to them.

## Formatting 
- If output from a tool call is to be returned to the user, all content should be output to the user verbatim as it appears in the output from the tool call. The only changes that should be made should be purely in eliminating unnecessary formatting, for example from a json dump. 

- All monetary amounts should be returned to 2 decimal places, even if it is a whole number. For example: $20.00.

traj.content:
# Telecom Agent Policy 

As a telecom agent, you can help users manage their customer details, devices, services, and billing. 

- Before taking any actions that modify the database, you must list out the actions to the user and get their explicit confirmation to proceed. 

- You should not provide any information, knowledge, or procedures not provided by the user or available tools, or give subjective recommendations or comments.

- You should only make one tool call at a time, and if you make a tool call, you should not respond to the user simultaneously. If you respond to the user, you should not make a tool call at the same time.

- You should deny user requests that are against this policy.

- You should transfer the user to a human agent if and only if the request cannot be handled within the scope of your actions.

- When the user asks you to verify something, make sure to read out the output returned with the relevant tool call you invoke.

- Do not suggest any upgrades, payments, or account modifications unless prompted explicitly to do so by the user.

## Domain Basic

- Each user has a profile containing customer id, name, demographics, address, account infromation, services, devices, and billing preferences.

- Each billing account has information including the customer id, account number, curent balance, last payment, next billing date, monthly charges, total monthly amount, payment history, and billing preferences (such as autopay and paperless).

- Each device has information including the category of the device (mobile_phone, phone, networking, tv, security), the manufacturer (Apple, Samsung, TechCorp, LG, Polycom, SecureTech, Google), and the model (Ex: iPhone 12, Galaxy S23). 

- Each service has information including the service id, the name of the plan, the category of the service (mobile, internet, internet, tv, security)

- Each support ticket has information including the ticket ID, the customer ID, the status of the ticket (open or closed) and its priority level (low, medium, high). Low is non-urgent, medium is standard, high is important, and urgent is critical (within 24 hours). Before creating a support ticket, determine as best as possible the urgency of the situation. Do not make more than one support ticket for the same issue.

## Services 

- When adding a service, you have the option to add devices to be associated with the service. When you do this, confirm with the user what devices they would like to add. The device ids can be found in the customer's information details. 

- When removing a service, all devices associated with that service will be removed from that service. 

- If the user seeks to switch out one service for another, you must remove the old service, then add the new service with the device ids that used to be associated with the old service.

## Authentication

- When authenticating a user with their email or phone number, make sure to read their customer ID back to them.

## Formatting 
- If output from a tool call is to be returned to the user, all content should be output to the user verbatim as it appears in the output from the tool call. The only changes that should be made should be purely in eliminating unnecessary formatting, for example from a json dump. 

- All monetary amounts should be returned to 2 decimal places, even if it is a whole number. For example: $20.00.
